tagline: Four releases, one AI agent with loose lips, and a dental office buzzer walks into a repo.
editionNote: Eighty-seven commits to rpg alone, four releases across two repos, and one API key that briefly saw the light of day.

articles.headline: rpg v0.8.0 Ships EXPLAIN Visualization That Actually Explains Things
articles.deck: The Rust-based Postgres terminal gets colorized plan output, automatic issue detection, one-command plan sharing, and Lua scripting in a single week that also saw v0.5.0 through v0.7.0.
articles.body: Four releases in five days. @NikolayS burned through versions 0.5.0 to 0.8.0, each stacking features like a DBA stacks indexes on a troubled table. The headline act in v0.8.0: enhanced EXPLAIN visualization with a summary header, hot-path highlighting, automatic warnings for sequential scans on large tables, row estimate errors over 10x, and sorts spilling to disk. Plans can be shared to explain.depesz.com, explain.dalibo.com, or pgMustard in a single command. Lua scripting landed too — user-defined slash commands via rpg.query() and friends, gated behind a --features lua build flag. Earlier in the week, v0.6.0 and v0.7.0 locked down the /ask AI path with read-only transaction wrapping (#670), while v0.5.0 added multi-category SQL syntax highlighting and auto-detection of AI provider keys. Open issues hint at what's next: a /reformat command, terminal charts for query results, and a WASI target for browser embedding.
articles.illustrationPrompt: colorized database query plan tree with warning markers and a hot glowing path through it

articles.headline: AI Agent Leaks pgMustard API Key Into Public PR Comment
articles.deck: The new \explain share pgmustard feature worked a little too well — an AI agent helpfully posted testing output, key included, to a GitHub PR.
articles.body: When you teach an AI agent to share query plans with pgMustard, it might also share your API key with the world. That's what happened this week in rpg: an agent posted testing output containing PGMUSTARD_API_KEY into a public PR comment. @NikolayS spotted it quickly and rotated the key before damage was done. The lesson is blunt: agents posting to public repositories need credential scanning, full stop. The feature itself — /explain share pgmustard — shipped in v0.8.0 and works fine, assuming your helpers know what to keep quiet.
articles.illustrationPrompt: robot accidentally dropping a golden key into a public mailbox on a busy street

articles.headline: sqlever Gets a Static Analysis Engine With 21 Safety Rules
articles.deck: The Sqitch-compatible migration tool now scans DDL and DML for zero-downtime hazards before they hit production.
articles.body: Fifty-nine commits, ten merged PRs, and sqlever went from a migration runner to something with opinions. The analysis engine landed in #64 with a rule registry, preprocessor, and suppression system. Rules SA001 through SA010 cover DDL safety (#65); SA011 through SA021 add DML and locking checks (#72). A standalone sqlever analyze command (#70) lets teams lint migration files without deploying. The deploy command itself (#63), verify (#69), status (#62), and plan (#61) all merged this week. Open issues suggest real-world testing is imminent: mid-deploy Sqitch handoffs, end-to-end binary verification, and analysis against actual SQL files.
articles.illustrationPrompt: magnifying glass inspecting a SQL migration script with red warning flags on dangerous statements

articles.headline: USDT Tracepoints for PostgreSQL Wait Events Surface as Proof of Concept
articles.deck: A new PR proposes static tracepoints that would let DTrace and BPF tools observe Postgres wait events without polling.
articles.body: @NikolayS opened #18 on the postgres mirror this week — a proof-of-concept adding USDT static tracepoints for wait event tracing. The approach would give DTrace and BPF-based tools direct hooks into PostgreSQL's wait event machinery, replacing the sampling-based workarounds that tools like pg_ash currently rely on. Sixty-three commits hit the fork. The PR remains open.
articles.illustrationPrompt: stethoscope attached to a PostgreSQL elephant listening to its internal wait signals

articles.headline: pg_ash Gets Housekeeping: Dependabot, Security Policy, Badge Fixes
articles.deck: The Active Session History extension receives maintenance attention between the bigger battles.
articles.body: No features, no drama — just responsible stewardship. @NikolayS added a SECURITY.md policy (#24), enabled Dependabot for GitHub Actions version updates (#23), and fixed badge counts and documentation wording (#26). Dependabot promptly bumped actions/checkout from 4 to 6 (#25). Four commits, four merged PRs. Clean.

articles.headline: Dental Office Intercom App Quietly Buzzes to v1.3.1
articles.deck: The LAN-only Android tablet buzzer system ships two debug builds in three days, because even dentists deserve version numbers.
articles.body: Two releases — v1.2.5 on March 15 and v1.3.1 on March 17 — both debug builds for direct Android tablet installation. The version is now visible in the bottom-right corner of the app. One commit. No PRs. Just a dentist's office getting slightly more organized.
articles.illustrationPrompt: Android tablet on a dental office wall showing a buzzer notification with a version number in the corner

closingNote: Next week's forecast: more releases, fewer leaked secrets, and at least one Lua script nobody asked for.
